era_context: The late 14th century in Europe was marked by the aftermath of the Black Death (1347–1353), which had killed 30–60% of Europe's population. The Hundred Years' War between England and France continued with intermittent campaigns. The Ottoman Empire was expanding in the Balkans and Anatolia. In East Asia, the Ming Dynasty (founded 1368) was consolidating control over China after overthrowing the Mongol Yuan. The Papacy remained in Avignon, and the Western Church faced growing institutional criticism.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records from this period)
Americas (no European contact; indigenous records limited)

events.title: Treaty of Troyes (English-Burgundian Alliance)
events.description: In 1370, English forces under John of Gaunt, Duke of Lancaster, negotiated or reinforced alliances with Burgundian powers as part of the broader Hundred Years' War strategy. The English sought to isolate France by securing Burgundian support and preventing French expansion in the Low Countries. These diplomatic maneuvers were part of the complex web of alliances that characterized the later phases of the Hundred Years' War. The exact nature and date of specific treaties in 1370 are somewhat unclear in sources, but the general pattern of English-Burgundian cooperation is well-attested.
key_figures: John of Gaunt, Duke of Lancaster (English commander)
Philip II, Duke of Burgundy (Burgundian ruler)
events.certainty_note: The general pattern of English-Burgundian alliance-building in the early 1370s is well-documented, but the precise date and nature of specific treaties in 1370 are less clear. Dating is approximate within ±2 years.
cross_references: Part of Hundred Years' War diplomacy (1337–1453)

events.title: Ming Dynasty Establishes Tributary Relations with Japan
events.description: In 1370, the Ming Dynasty began establishing tributary relations with Japan, particularly with the Ashikaga shogunate. These diplomatic missions and tributary arrangements were part of the Ming's broader strategy to establish regional hegemony and secure trade routes. The tributary system allowed the Ming to project power and prestige without direct military conquest. By 1370, the Ming had begun sending diplomatic missions to Japan, establishing the framework for the tributary relationships that would characterize Ming foreign policy for centuries. These relations also facilitated trade and cultural exchange between China and Japan.
key_figures: Ashikaga Yoshiakira (Japanese shogun, r. 1358–1367, but his successors continued relations)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Japanese chronicles and records (Ashikaga period sources)
Historiographic consensus on Ming-Japanese tributary relations

events.certainty_note: The general timeline of Ming tributary relations with Japan is well-established, with initial missions in the early 1370s. The precise date of first missions in 1370 is approximate within ±1 year.
cross_references: Part of Ming Dynasty foreign policy (1368–1644)
Related to Ming tributary system expansion

events.title: Death of Urban V (Pope)
events.description: Pope Urban V died on 19 December 1370 in Avignon. Urban V (born Guillaume de Grimoard) had served as pope from 1362 to 1370. His papacy was marked by attempts to reform the Church and to return the papal seat from Avignon to Rome, which he briefly accomplished in 1367 before returning to Avignon in 1370. His death marked the end of a significant reform effort and the continuation of the Avignon Papacy. Urban V was succeeded by Gregory XI, who would eventually move the papal seat back to Rome in 1377, ending the Avignon Papacy.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Urban V's papacy and death

events.certainty_note: The date of Urban V's death (19 December 1370) is well-attested in papal records and contemporary chronicles. His papacy and significance are uncontested.

events.title: Hundred Years' War: English Campaigns in France
events.description: In 1370, the Hundred Years' War between England and France continued with ongoing military campaigns. The war was characterized by intermittent periods of active conflict and truces. In 1370, English forces under John of Gaunt and other commanders were engaged in campaigns in France, particularly in Aquitaine and other English-held territories. The war was economically draining both kingdoms and contributing to social unrest. The period saw the rise of mercenary companies and the increasing use of longbowmen by English forces.

sources.name: English and French royal records and chronicles
Historiographic consensus on Hundred Years' War timeline

events.certainty_note: The general state of the war in 1370 is well-documented, though specific military engagements of this year are not always clearly distinguished in sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of 1370 as a specific year in European history is often overstated. While Urban V's death is a confirmed religious landmark, the year itself was not marked by major political or military turning points in England or France. The Hundred Years' War continued in a relatively static phase, and Ottoman expansion, while significant over the long term, did not reach a critical juncture in 1370 specifically. Some sources conflate events of the early 1370s without precise annual differentiation.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1370 is moderate for Western European events (particularly Urban V's death) and East Asian events (Ming tributary relations), but sparse for other regions. Western European events are well-documented through chronicles and administrative records. East Asian events (Ming foreign policy) are well-documented in Chinese sources but less accessible to Western historiography. The year is marked by one significant religious event (Urban V's death) but is otherwise relatively quiet in terms of major turning points.

graph_edges.note: Urban V's death in 1370 led to Gregory XI's papacy, which ended the Avignon Papacy in 1377
Ming tributary relations established in 1370 provided the framework for later diplomatic and trade relations
English-Burgundian diplomatic maneuvers continued alongside the broader Hundred Years' War conflict
English military campaigns in 1370 continued through the early 1370s, culminating in the naval defeat at La Rochelle in 1372
